justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Confidence calibration is systematic — every row in every table carries an explicit confidence tag (CONFIRMED/INFERRED/ASSUMED/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW). (2) High-confidence sections clearly identify date range params, endpoint structure, and download behavior. (3) Low-confidence sections explicitly flag 'usage' as undefined with a prominent WARNING block and repeated open questions. (4) Partial escalation to Bird is present — open_questions #1 names the gap, domain_rules table warns against inventing metrics, and the recommended action tells Shaq to return to Bird with concrete metric names. (5) Shaq is NOT fully blocked — the brief explicitly recommends starting with endpoint scaffolding and stub aggregation, making the escalation partial. (6) No report content is invented — the brief repeatedly refuses to define 'usage' metrics. (7) Pippen's async recommendation gets a dedicated section with a concrete resolution path and pragmatic fallback.

observations.text: Exceptionally granular confidence calibration — not just per-section but per-row in every table, with source attribution (Bird, MJ, Pippen)
The WARNING block in domain_rules is well-placed and strongly worded: 'Do not invent metrics during implementation'
Async vs sync tension is not just noted but given a full resolution framework with concrete thresholds (30-day range heuristic, 90-day cap fallback)
Open questions are prioritized and actionable — #1 (what is usage) is correctly identified as the most critical gap
Acceptance criteria are explicitly labeled as 'MJ-derived proxy criteria' rather than presented as authoritative, preventing Shaq from treating them as validated requirements
Authorization scoping (#6 in open questions) is caught as an implicit gap from Bird's language — good inferential reading

The async/sync section header says 'DO NOT IMPLEMENT UNTIL RESOLVED' which slightly over-blocks Shaq — the pragmatic fallback at the bottom contradicts this hard stop, creating minor tension in the brief's own guidance
